 {"id":"wa-1pe.3","title":"Implement `wa workflow run --dry-run`: preview all workflow steps without execution","description":"
# wa workflow run --dry-run Implementation

## Purpose
Show users exactly what steps a workflow would execute, allowing them to understand and verify workflow behavior before running.

## Output Format
```
DRY RUN - No changes will be made

Workflow: handle_compaction
Target: Pane 7 (codex @ /home/user/project)

Pre-checks:
  ✓ Compaction anchor found in pane tail
  ✓ Pane not in AltScreen
  ✓ No recent output gaps
  ✓ Policy: workflow execution allowed

Steps that would execute:
  1. Acquire workflow lock for pane 7
  2. Stabilize: wait for tail stability (no new deltas for N polls; max 2s)
  3. Send: "Please re-read AGENTS.md..."
  4. Verify: wait for prompt boundary
  5. Mark event handled

Estimated steps: 5

To execute for real:
  wa workflow run handle_compaction --pane 7
```

## Implementation Steps
1. Load workflow definition
2. Resolve target pane and verify preconditions
3. Call workflow.plan_steps() to get step list
4. Evaluate each step's policy requirements
5. Build and display step plan report

## Key Considerations
- Don't acquire locks during dry-run
- Show estimated step count
- Highlight steps that might fail policy checks
- Show what event would be marked handled

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] wa workflow run --dry-run lists all steps
- [ ] Pre-checks shown with pass/fail
- [ ] Each step described with its action
- [ ] Policy-gated steps highlighted
- [ ] JSON output for robot mode

## Testing
- Unit tests for dry_run flag propagation and no-side-effect guards.
- Integration tests: verify no DB/lock/pane mutation under dry-run.
- E2E: add a dry-run scenario with verbose logs and artifacts.

 {"id":"wa-1pe.4","title":"Implement `wa approve --dry-run`: preview approval token validation and grant","description":"
# wa approve --dry-run Implementation

## Purpose
Let users verify that an approval code is valid and see what action it would permit before actually granting approval.

## Output Format
```
DRY RUN - No changes will be made

Approval Token Validation:
  Token: abc123
  Status: ✓ Valid
  Created: 2026-01-18T14:30:00Z
  Expires: 2026-01-18T15:30:00Z

Associated Action:
  Type: SendText
  Pane: 9 (codex @ /project)
  Content: "sudo rm -rf /tmp/cache"
  Reason: RequireApproval (destructive pattern match)

Would grant:
  - Allow-once permission for this specific action
  - Action will execute immediately after approval
  - Audit log entry will be created

To approve for real:
  wa approve abc123
```

## Implementation
1. Parse approval token
2. Look up pending approval request
3. Validate token hasn't expired
4. Display associated action details
5. Don't actually modify approval state

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Shows approval token validation status
- [ ] Displays the action that would be permitted
- [ ] Shows expiration warning if near expiry
- [ ] JSON output for robot mode

## Testing
- Unit tests for dry_run flag propagation and no-side-effect guards.
- Integration tests: verify no DB/lock/pane mutation under dry-run.
- E2E: add a dry-run scenario with verbose logs and artifacts.

 {"id":"wa-1pe.5","title":"Dry-run tests: verify no state modifications, output correctness, error paths","description":"
# Dry-Run Testing Suite

## Purpose
Ensure dry-run mode never modifies state and produces accurate predictions.

## Test Categories

### 1. No State Modification Tests
- Dry-run send: DB unchanged, no WezTerm CLI calls
- Dry-run workflow: No locks acquired, no step logs
- Dry-run approve: Approval state unchanged

### 2. Output Correctness Tests
- Target resolution matches actual resolution
- Policy evaluation matches actual evaluation
- Step plan matches actual execution order

### 3. Error Path Tests
- Invalid pane: helpful error in dry-run output
- Policy denial: shows what would block
- Expired approval: shows expiry clearly

### 4. Format Tests
- TTY output is human-readable
- JSON output is valid and complete
- All fields present in both formats

### 5. Contract Tests
- Dry-run output fields match execution fields
- No drift between preview and reality

## Testing Approach
- Mock WezTerm CLI to detect any calls
- Compare dry-run output to actual execution logs
- Snapshot tests for output stability

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 100% coverage of dry-run code paths
- [ ] Verified no state modification in any test
- [ ] Output snapshots for regression detection
- [ ] Error message quality verified

+{"id":"wa-1pygr","title":"Zellij WASM plugin system analysis — extensibility model for FrankenTerm","description":"# Zellij WASM Plugin System Analysis

## Why this matters
Zellij's WASM plugin system is its most distinctive feature. FrankenTerm currently uses Lua (inherited from WezTerm) and MCP for extensibility. WASM plugins could offer:
- Language-agnostic extensibility (Rust, Go, Python compiled to WASM)
- Sandboxed execution (no filesystem/network access unless granted)
- Better performance than Lua for compute-heavy plugins
- Hot-reloadable without server restart

## What to analyze

### Plugin API surface
- What host functions does Zellij expose to plugins?
- How do plugins interact with panes, tabs, sessions?
- Can plugins create/destroy/modify panes? Subscribe to events?
- What data flows between host and plugin (and in what format)?

### WASM runtime integration
- Which WASM runtime (wasmtime/wasmer) and why?
- How are plugins loaded, instantiated, and managed?
- Memory model: how is data shared between host and guest?
- Performance: what's the overhead of host↔plugin calls?

### Plugin lifecycle
- How are plugins discovered, installed, enabled/disabled?
- Can plugins be hot-reloaded?
- How does Zellij handle plugin crashes (isolation)?

### Capability/permission model
- Can plugins be granted selective permissions?
- How does Zellij sandbox filesystem/network access?
- Compare to asupersync's Cx capability model

## FrankenTerm implications
Could WASM plugins replace or complement:
- Lua scripts (currently on hot path, performance concern per wa-5nd5)
- MCP tool handlers (currently feature-gated)
- Custom capture/analysis logic

## Acceptance criteria
- Plugin API surface documented
- WASM runtime integration analyzed
- Plugin lifecycle and permissions documented
- Comparison with FrankenTerm's Lua/MCP approach
- Go/no-go recommendation on WASM plugins for FrankenTerm","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T15:53:56.361172Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T15:53:56.361172Z"}
 {"id":"wa-1q7m","title":"FTUI-09.3 Create ratatui/crossterm decommission and removal plan","description":"## Background\nLegacy stack retirement must be explicit to avoid long-term dual maintenance cost.\n\n## Deliverables\n- module-by-module removal checklist\n- dependency removal sequence and guardrails\n- post-removal verification checklist\n\n## Acceptance Criteria\n- no ambiguous ownership remains between stacks\n- decommission sequence can be executed safely.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:10:52.565544062Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-09T05:37:41.022947851Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T05:37:41.022813471Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1q7m","depends_on_id":"wa-3ue0","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:10:52.597138979Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-1q7m","depends_on_id":"wa-wzft","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:24:00.363889153Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"}]}
 {"id":"wa-1qr1","title":"FTUI-07.3.a Deterministic PTY fixture seed/timing strategy","description":"## Background
PTY E2E suites are only trustworthy if timing and input generation are deterministic and anti-flake by design.

## Deliverables
- deterministic seed policy for PTY scenario generation
- timing/control strategy that removes sleep-based nondeterminism
- anti-flake constraints (timeouts, retries, jitter controls, artifact requirements)

## Acceptance Criteria
- repeated runs produce stable outcomes and comparable logs across environments
- suite guidance specifies required transcript/log metadata for debugging
- strategy is adopted by FTUI PTY scenarios and referenced from CI gate bead.,","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:14:37.597376381Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-09T05:03:56.95358251Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T05:03:56.953454703Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1qr1","depends_on_id":"wa-3gii","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:14:37.616084937Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-1qr1","depends_on_id":"wa-bjvg","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:25:34.127403507Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"}]}
 {"id":"wa-1qz1","title":"[EPIC] Principled Intelligence Engine — Mathematically-grounded decision systems","description":"## Goal
Create a Principled Intelligence Engine (PIE) that replaces FrankenTerm's heuristic-based decision-making systems with mathematically-principled approaches, giving formal guarantees and explainable decisions.

## Background & Motivation
FrankenTerm's current decision-making (polling rates, backpressure thresholds, pane classification, state detection) is driven by hardcoded heuristics:
- tailer.rs: Fixed 1.5x backoff multiplier for polling
- backpressure.rs: Hardcoded thresholds (0.50/0.75 capture, 0.60/0.80 write) with fixed 2s hysteresis
- patterns.rs: Regex-only detection with fixed 0.6 confidence fallback
- watchdog.rs: Fixed heartbeat thresholds (5s/15s/30s/120s)
- degradation.rs: Discrete levels with no continuous recovery modeling

These heuristics work but leave significant performance and reliability on the table. By applying probability theory, information theory, and survival analysis, we can build systems that:
1. Give formal mathematical guarantees (not just empirical observations)
2. Are fully explainable (evidence ledgers showing WHY each decision was made)
3. Adapt automatically (no parameter tuning needed)
4. Degrade gracefully (work with whatever data is available)

## Design Philosophy (Alien Artifact Quality)
Every PIE component must have ALL of:
1. Mathematical rigor — grounded in formal theory
2. Complete explainability — evidence ledgers, Bayes factors
3. Formal safety guarantees — provable error bounds
4. Graceful degradation — works with partial data
5. Operational excellence — fast, no resource leaks
6. Beautiful UX — galaxy-brain transparency cards showing the math

## Scope
This epic covers 7 principled replacements for heuristic systems, plus test suites:
1. Survival/hazard model for mux server health prediction
2. BOCPD for agent state transition detection
3. VOI-optimal capture scheduling
4. Conformal prediction for resource forecasting
5. Bayesian evidence ledger for pane classification
6. Continuous backpressure severity function
7. Adaptive watchdog thresholds via Kalman filter

## Relationship to Existing Beads
- Enhances bd-9dp (tiered rates) — VOI scheduler is the principled replacement
- Enhances wa-3bin (smart priority) — Bayesian evidence ledger replaces EWMA+thresholds
- Enhances backpressure.rs — continuous severity replaces 4-tier FSM
- Enhances watchdog.rs — Kalman filter replaces fixed thresholds
- Complements patterns.rs — BOCPD catches what regex can't
- Integrates with wa-rsaf (session persistence) — survival model triggers proactive saves

## Dependencies
None — standalone epic. Cross-references existing performance and session persistence beads.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 7 components implemented with formal mathematical foundations
- Evidence ledgers for every classification decision
- Galaxy-brain transparency cards accessible via wa robot API
- Comprehensive unit tests (100+) with statistical validation
- Integration tests proving PIE components work with existing pipeline
- Performance: no more than 5% overhead vs current heuristic approach
- Documentation with mathematical specifications","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:23:45.195392Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-09T21:23:45.195392Z"}
